note_data.title: Capacitance
keyPoints: **Capacitance** is defined as the charge stored per unit potential difference across a capacitor
A **capacitor** consists of two conducting plates separated by an insulator (dielectric)
The **capacitance** of a parallel plate capacitor is given by C = Оµв‚ЂОµбµЈA/d where A is plate area and d is separation
During **charging**, current decreases exponentially and follows I = Iв‚Ђe^(-t/RC)
During **discharging**, charge decreases exponentially following Q = Qв‚Ђe^(-t/RC)
The **time constant** П„ = RC is the time taken for charge to fall to 1/e (37%) of its initial value
**Energy stored** in a capacitor is given by W = ВЅQV = ВЅCVВІ = ВЅQВІ/C
A **dielectric** increases capacitance by a factor of ОµбµЈ (relative permittivity)
Capacitors in **parallel**: C = Cв‚Ѓ + Cв‚‚ + Cв‚ѓ

definitions.definition: The charge stored per unit potential difference across a capacitor
The time taken for the charge on a capacitor to fall to 1/e of its initial value during discharge
An insulating material placed between capacitor plates that increases the capacitance
The ratio of the permittivity of a material to the permittivity of free space

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Start with the given equation and manipulate algebraically to reach the required result
Show all working steps with correct units and appropriate significant figures

modelAnswer.question: A 470 ОјF capacitor is charged to 12 V and then discharged through a 2.2 kО© resistor. Calculate (a) the initial energy stored (b) the time constant (c) the charge remaining after 2.0 s.
modelAnswer.answer:
(a) W = ВЅCVВІ = ВЅ Г— 470 Г— 10вЃ»вЃ¶ Г— 12ВІ = 0.034 J
(b) П„ = RC = 2.2 Г— 10Ві Г— 470 Г— 10вЃ»вЃ¶ = 1.03 s
(c) Qв‚Ђ = CV = 470 Г— 10вЃ»вЃ¶ Г— 12 = 5.64 Г— 10вЃ»Ві C
Q = Qв‚Ђe^(-t/RC) = 5.64 Г— 10вЃ»Ві Г— e^(-2.0/1.03) = 7.4 Г— 10вЃ»вЃґ C

commonMistakes: Never say 'stores current' вЂ” always say 'stores charge' when describing capacitors
Students often confuse charging and discharging equations вЂ” check if Qв‚Ђ is initial or final value
Never use П„ = 1/RC вЂ” the correct time constant is П„ = RC
Students forget to convert units (ОјF to F, kО© to О©) leading to incorrect orders of magnitude
Never say 'completely discharged' вЂ” capacitors approach zero charge asymptotically
examinerTips: Capacitance calculations appear in every Paper 2 вЂ” practice exponential decay graphs
Always check units in your final answer вЂ” charge in C, energy in J, time in s
Draw I-t and Q-t graphs clearly showing exponential curves, not straight lines
Time constant questions often ask for 1/e, 1/2 or specific percentages вЂ” know all forms
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Exponential decay graph showing charge Q against time t, with time constant П„ marked where Q = Qв‚Ђ/e, and axes clearly labelled with Qв‚Ђ at t = 0
